sections.title: Overview
sections.content: Finger thoracostomy is an advanced chest decompression procedure used in selected critically unwell trauma patients, particularly when tension pneumothorax is strongly suspected and the patient is ventilated, peri-arrest, or has failed needle decompression. It is more reliable than needle decompression but requires appropriate training, scope, and scene control.

sections.title: Procedure
sections.content:
1. Confirm the clinical need for immediate decompression and that the procedure is within scope, indicated, and supported by the environment

2. Identify the 4th or 5th intercostal space around the anterior to mid-axillary line on the affected side

3. Prepare the site rapidly, maintaining attention to overall airway, breathing, and circulatory priorities

4. Make an incision over the superior rib margin and bluntly dissect into the pleural space with the appropriate instrument

5. Enter the pleural space, confirm decompression clinically, and sweep with a finger to maintain patency if required

6. Leave the opening non-occluded and continue close reassessment during transport

sections.title: Key Points
sections.content:
- Incise and dissect over the superior rib margin to reduce risk to the intercostal neurovascular bundle
- The goal is decompression and patency, not a formal definitive drain
- Bilateral decompression may be required in selected traumatic arrest or severe bilateral chest injury scenarios
- Document the indication, side, response, and any ongoing concerns such as continued deterioration or re-tension
